era_context: The High Middle Ages; the Investiture Controversy between the papacy and the Holy Roman Empire dominates Western European politics. Pope Gregory VII is in his final years (died May 1085, but his influence persists through 1086). The Norman Conquest of England is now two decades past; William the Conqueror is aging and will die in 1087. In the Islamic world, the Seljuk Empire under Malik Shah is at its height. The Northern Song Dynasty in China faces military pressures. Japan is in the Heian period.

events.title: Domesday Book Completed in England
events.description: King William I the Conqueror commissioned the Domesday Book, a comprehensive survey of English lands, resources, and population. The survey was conducted in 1085–1086 and completed by 1086. The Domesday Book recorded landholdings, livestock, mills, and other resources for taxation and administrative purposes. It represents one of the most detailed administrative records of medieval Europe and provides invaluable information about 11th-century English society. The survey was unprecedented in its scope and detail.
key_figures: William I the Conqueror (King of England)
events.certainty_note: The Domesday Book is a contemporary primary source, completed in 1086. Its existence and purpose are well-documented.

events.title: Pope Victor III Elected; Papacy Remains Contested
events.description: Following Gregory VII's death in May 1085, the cardinals elected Desiderius of Monte Cassino as Pope Victor III in May 1086. However, Victor III's papacy was weak and contested. The antipope Clement III, installed by Emperor Henry IV, retained control of Rome and significant support. Victor III's health was poor, and he struggled to assert papal authority. His papacy lasted only until 1087, when he died and was succeeded by Urban II. The Investiture Controversy continued unabated.
key_figures: Pope Victor III (Desiderius of Monte Cassino)

sources.name: Bonizo of Sutri, Liber ad amicum

events.certainty_note: Victor III's election in May 1086 is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Battle of Sagrajas (Zallaqa): Almoravid Victory Over Christian Spain
events.description: In 1086, the Almoravid dynasty from North Africa defeated a coalition of Christian Spanish kingdoms at the Battle of Sagrajas (also called Zallaqa). The Christian forces, led by King Alfonso VI of Castile, were decisively defeated by the Almoravid army under Yusuf ibn Tashfin. This battle marked a turning point in the Reconquista, halting Christian advances and leading to increased Almoravid intervention in Iberian affairs. The Almoravids would eventually conquer much of Muslim Spain and establish their own rule.
key_figures: Alfonso VI (King of Castile and León)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Sagrajas in 1086 is well-attested in both Islamic and Christian sources, though some details are disputed.

events.title: Malik Shah Consolidates Seljuk Control of Anatolia and Persia
events.description: Sultan Malik Shah of the Seljuk Empire continued to consolidate his control over vast territories stretching from Anatolia to Persia. Under the guidance of his vizier Nizam al-Mulk, the Seljuk Empire reached the height of its power and territorial extent. The Seljuk court at Isfahan became a center of learning and culture. Malik Shah's reign represented the zenith of Seljuk power before the empire fragmented after his death in 1092.
events.certainty_note: Malik Shah's reign and territorial consolidation are well-attested in later Islamic sources, though specific events of 1086 are not always precisely documented.
cross_references: 1092-001 (Death of Nizam al-Mulk and Malik Shah)

events.title: Northern Song Dynasty Experiences Economic Strain
events.description: The Northern Song Dynasty under Emperor Shenzong continued to experience economic strain from military expenditures and the costs of maintaining a large bureaucracy. The Song faced military pressures from the Khitan Liao Dynasty in the north and the Tangut Western Xia kingdom in the northwest. These conflicts drained the Song treasury and prompted ongoing debates at court about taxation, military strategy, and resource allocation.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: Song economic conditions and military pressures are documented in Chinese sources, though specific events of 1086 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The Domesday Book is sometimes portrayed as a complete and accurate census of England, but modern scholarship emphasizes its limitations. It excluded some regions (notably the far north), and its valuations and classifications were sometimes inconsistent or inaccurate. The Battle of Sagrajas is sometimes characterized as a decisive Almoravid victory that halted the Reconquista permanently, but modern scholarship suggests the battle's significance was more limited; Christian advances resumed in subsequent decades. The characterization of Malik Shah's reign as a period of unambiguous consolidation is complicated by evidence of internal tensions within the Seljuk court.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1086 is moderate but unevenly distributed. English sources are particularly rich, reflecting the importance of the Domesday Book and the Norman Conquest to English history. Western European ecclesiastical sources document the papal succession crisis. Islamic sources document the Battle of Sagrajas and Seljuk affairs, though with different emphases. Chinese sources are detailed but focus on court politics. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political, military, and administrative events. Economic, social, and cultural history must be inferred from broader patterns. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and Central Asia are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: The Domesday Book was completed shortly before William I's death in 1087
The Almoravid victory at Sagrajas led to increased Almoravid intervention in Iberian affairs
Victor III's weak papacy led to Urban II's election in 1088
